title: Git Workflows (GitFlow, Trunk-based)
summary: Explore different Git branching strategies used by teams. Compare GitFlow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development. Understand when to use each approach and how they affect CI/CD pipelines.

resources.title: Git Flow vs GitHub Flow vs Trunk-based

questions.questionText: In GitFlow, which branch contains the latest production release?
questions.explanation: In GitFlow, 'main' (or 'master') always reflects the production-ready state. The 'develop' branch is where features are integrated for the next release.

questions.questionText: What is the core principle of trunk-based development?
questions.explanation: Trunk-based development advocates that developers integrate small, frequent changes directly to the main branch (trunk). Short-lived feature branches (< 1-2 days) may be used but long-lived branches are avoided.

options.text: Developers commit small, frequent changes directly to main
Only one developer works on the codebase at a time
All changes must go through a release branch

questions.questionText: In GitHub Flow, what is the primary mechanism for code review?
questions.explanation: GitHub Flow uses pull requests as the primary mechanism for code review. Developers create a branch, open a PR, get reviews, and merge when approved.

tasks.title: Simulate a GitFlow Workflow
tasks.description:
1. Create a new repository: `mkdir gitflow-demo && cd gitflow-demo && git init`
2. Create initial commit on main: `echo 'v1.0' > version.txt && git add . && git commit -m 'Initial release v1.0'`
3. Create the develop branch: `git switch -c develop`
4. Create a feature branch: `git switch -c feature/user-auth`
5. Add a feature file, commit, and merge back to develop:
   ```
   echo 'auth module' > auth.sh
   git add . && git commit -m 'Add user authentication'
   git switch develop && git merge --no-ff feature/user-auth
   ```
6. Create a release branch: `git switch -c release/1.1`
7. Bump version: `echo 'v1.1' > version.txt && git add . && git commit -m 'Bump version to 1.1'`
8. Merge to main and tag: `git switch main && git merge --no-ff release/1.1 && git tag v1.1`
9. Merge back to develop: `git switch develop && git merge --no-ff release/1.1`
10. View the full graph: `git log --oneline --graph --all`
hints: Use --no-ff (no fast-forward) to preserve branch history in the merge
Tags mark release points: git tag -a v1.1 -m 'Release 1.1'

tasks.title: Practice GitHub Flow
tasks.description:
1. In an existing GitHub repository, create an issue describing a feature
2. Create a branch named after the issue: `git switch -c feature/42-add-logging`
3. Make your changes in small, logical commits
4. Push the branch: `git push -u origin feature/42-add-logging`
5. Open a Pull Request on GitHub:
   - Reference the issue in the description (e.g., 'Closes #42')
   - Add a clear description of changes
   - Request a review (or review your own for practice)
6. After approval, merge via the GitHub UI
7. Pull the updated main locally: `git pull origin main`
8. Delete the remote branch: `git push origin --delete feature/42-add-logging`
9. Delete the local branch: `git branch -d feature/42-add-logging`
10. Compare this workflow to GitFlow — which feels simpler?
hints: Using 'Closes #42' in a PR description automatically closes the issue when the PR is merged
GitHub Flow works best with CI/CD because every merge to main should be deployable
